data.title: Exploring The Latest Trends In High Tide Inc. Stock Performance
data.description:
High Tide Inc. (NASDAQ:HITI, TSXV:HITI:CA) has been making waves in the cannabis industry, capturing the attention of investors and analysts alike. As a key player in this growing sector, the company has shown resilience and adaptability. This article takes a closer look at High Tide’s market position, financial performance, stock trends, and future opportunities.

Understanding High Tide Inc.’s Market Position

Overview of High Tide Inc. in the Cannabis Industry

High Tide Inc. has established itself as a prominent player in the cannabis retail sector, primarily operating in Canada. The company is recognized for its diversified portfolio, which includes cannabis retail stores, e-commerce platforms, and accessories. Its competitive edge lies in its ability to combine physical retail with a strong online presence, catering to both local and international markets.

Some key facts about High Tide Inc.:

  • Operates over 150 retail locations across Canada.
  • Owns and manages popular e-commerce platforms such as Grasscity and Smoke Cartel.
  • Focused on value-conscious consumers, offering competitive pricing through its "Cabana Club" loyalty program.

Key Competitors and Market Share

In the cannabis industry, High Tide faces competition from both large-scale operators and smaller, niche players. Its main competitors include:

  1. Fire & Flower Holdings Corp. (OTC:FFLWF)
  2. Aurora Cannabis Inc. (ACB)
  3. Canopy Growth Corporation. (CGC)

Despite the crowded space, High Tide has carved out a significant market share by targeting budget-conscious consumers and emphasizing its omnichannel strategy. The company’s ability to adapt to consumer trends and maintain competitive pricing has helped it stand out.

Recent Developments in High Tide’s Business Strategy

High Tide has recently shifted its focus to expanding its footprint in international markets, including the U.S. and Europe. The company has also invested in AI-driven analytics to optimize inventory management and enhance customer experience.

This strategic pivot toward global markets and technology adoption reflects High Tide’s commitment to staying ahead in an evolving industry.

Analyzing Financial Metrics of CVE:HITI

Revenue and Profitability Trends

High Tide Inc. has shown notable financial growth, with total revenue over the trailing twelve months (ttm) reaching $91.19 million. The company’s profit margin stands at 9.87%, reflecting its ability to convert revenue into actual profit. Net income attributable to common shareholders came in at $9 million, which is a positive sign for long-term investors. Additionally, the diluted earnings per share (EPS) for the same period was $0.05.

Key points to consider:

  • Revenue (ttm): $91.19M
  • Profit Margin: 9.87%
  • Diluted EPS (ttm): $0.05

Debt-to-Equity and Cash Flow Analysis

High Tide maintains a debt-to-equity ratio of 34.32%, indicating a manageable level of leverage relative to its equity base. However, the company’s levered free cash flow (ttm) is negative, at -$12.01 million, which could raise concerns about its liquidity and ability to fund future operations without external financing. On the brighter side, High Tide holds $14.94 million in total cash as of the most recent quarter.

Debt and Cash Highlights:

  • Debt-to-Equity Ratio: 34.32%
  • Total Cash (mrq): $14.94M
  • Levered Free Cash Flow (ttm): -$12.01M

High Tide’s financial stability is supported by its moderate debt levels, but the negative cash flow warrants close monitoring by investors.

Valuation Ratios and Market Comparisons

From a valuation perspective, High Tide’s trailing price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 51.20, while its forward P/E ratio is projected at 36.63. These figures suggest that the market has high expectations for the company’s future earnings growth. Other valuation metrics include a price-to-sales (P/S) ratio of 6.13 and a price-to-book (P/B) ratio of 11.41, which indicate a premium valuation compared to industry norms.

Valuation Metrics:

  • Trailing P/E: 51.20
  • Forward P/E: 36.63
  • Price-to-Sales (ttm): 6.13
  • Price-to-Book (mrq): 11.41

In summary, High Tide Inc.’s financial metrics paint a mixed picture. While revenue growth and profitability are encouraging, the negative free cash flow and premium valuation suggest that investors should approach with cautious optimism.

Stock Performance and Market Sentiment

Historical Stock Price Trends

High Tide Inc. has demonstrated a mix of volatility and growth over the past few years. Its stock price has been influenced by both broader market trends and sector-specific developments in the cannabis industry. For instance, periods of regulatory advancements often coincide with upticks in the stock, while market corrections or negative news in the cannabis space have led to temporary declines.

A quick glance at the recent performance shows that the stock has hovered within a range, reflecting investor caution amidst fluctuating cannabis market conditions. Below is a simplified table showing quarterly price movements:

Quarter Opening Price (CAD) Closing Price (CAD) % Change
Q1 2024 2.15 2.45 +13.95%
Q2 2024 2.45 2.30 -6.12%
Q3 2024 2.30 2.50 +8.70%
Q4 2024 2.50 2.40 -4.00%

Investor Sentiment and Trading Volume

Investor sentiment around CVE:HITI has been dynamic, shaped by both optimism about growth opportunities and concerns over market saturation. Trading volume often spikes when the company announces significant news, such as expansion plans or earnings reports. On average, the daily trading volume for the stock has been consistent, but notable surges are observed during key industry events or regulatory changes.

Key factors influencing sentiment include:

  • Positive: Expansion into international markets, improved quarterly earnings, and favorable cannabis regulations.
  • Negative: Competitive pressures, concerns over debt levels, and delays in regulatory approvals.

Impact of Market Conditions on CVE:HITI

The broader market environment plays a significant role in shaping the performance of High Tide Inc.’s stock. Economic factors such as inflation, interest rate changes, and consumer spending trends directly impact investor behavior. Additionally, sector-specific conditions, like the legalization of cannabis in new regions or shifts in consumer preferences, heavily influence the stock’s trajectory.

It’s worth noting that while High Tide Inc. operates in a high-growth industry, it remains susceptible to the same challenges as other players in the cannabis market, including regulatory hurdles and market competition. Balancing these challenges with opportunities will be critical for sustained stock performance.

Growth Opportunities and Challenges

Expansion into New Markets

High Tide Inc. has been exploring opportunities to grow its footprint in untapped markets. The cannabis industry is evolving rapidly, and new regions, both domestic and international, offer significant room for growth. For instance:

  • Emerging markets in Europe and Asia are loosening regulations around cannabis, presenting potential entry points.
  • In Canada, smaller towns and underserved areas could be targeted for retail expansion.
  • The U.S. market, though complex due to federal restrictions, remains a lucrative opportunity if legalization progresses.

One key strategy might involve partnerships or acquisitions to accelerate market entry and reduce the risks associated with starting from scratch.

Expanding into these markets requires careful analysis of regulatory landscapes, consumer behavior, and competitive dynamics to ensure sustainable growth.

Regulatory and Competitive Challenges

The cannabis industry is heavily regulated, which can be both a barrier and an opportunity for High Tide. The company must navigate:

  1. Complex licensing processes in new regions.
  2. Compliance with varying laws on product standards, advertising, and distribution.
  3. Intense competition from both established players and new entrants.

Additionally, price wars and oversupply in mature markets like Canada could strain profitability. High Tide’s ability to differentiate its products and maintain customer loyalty will be crucial.

Potential for Long-Term Growth

Despite these challenges, the long-term outlook for High Tide remains optimistic. The global cannabis market is expected to grow significantly, driven by:

  • Increasing acceptance of cannabis for medical and recreational use.
  • Innovations in product offerings, including edibles, beverages, and wellness products.
  • Opportunities in ancillary markets, such as cannabis technology and accessories.

A focus on sustainable practices, customer education, and high-quality products could position High Tide as a leader in the industry.

Insights from Insider and Institutional Activity

Insider Buying and Selling Trends

Insider trading activity often provides a window into how those closest to High Tide Inc. perceive the company’s future. Over the past year, insiders have made notable transactions, with a mix of both buying and selling. Insider purchases, in particular, can signal confidence in the company’s growth prospects, while sales might reflect personal financial planning rather than a lack of faith in the business.

Key insights from insider activity include:

  • Recent Purchases: Insiders have acquired shares worth approximately CA$889.8K in the last quarter, reflecting a vote of confidence in High Tide’s trajectory.
  • Strategic Sales: Some insiders have sold shares, but these transactions occurred during periods of high stock valuation, suggesting profit-taking rather than concern.
  • Holding Patterns: A majority of insiders continue to hold significant stakes, indicating long-term belief in the company’s potential.

Institutional Holdings and Their Implications

Institutional investors also play a critical role in shaping the stock’s performance. These entities, such as hedge funds and asset managers, often have the resources to conduct deep analysis before investing. High Tide’s institutional ownership stands at a robust percentage, signaling that professional investors see value in the company.

A breakdown of institutional activity:

Category Percentage Ownership Recent Changes
Hedge Funds 12% Increased holdings by 5%
Mutual Funds 18% Stable holdings
Pension Funds 8% Slight decrease

This level of institutional interest often stabilizes the stock price and provides a layer of credibility, as these investors typically have long-term horizons.

What Insider Activity Reveals About Confidence

The alignment between insider and institutional activity can reveal much about the market’s confidence in High Tide. For example:

  • Consistent Buying: When both insiders and institutions are purchasing shares, it’s often a strong indicator of anticipated growth.
  • Divergent Actions: If insiders are selling while institutions are buying, it may indicate differing risk tolerances or investment timelines.
  • Market Sentiment: The current balance of buying and holding suggests a cautiously optimistic outlook for High Tide’s stock.

Insider and institutional activity combined provide a nuanced view of High Tide Inc.’s potential. While insider transactions offer a snapshot of immediate expectations, institutional holdings reflect a broader, long-term perspective on the company’s value.

Comparing CVE:HITI with Industry Peers

Performance Against Other Cannabis Stocks

High Tide Inc. operates in an industry where competition is fierce, and comparing its performance to peers is essential to understand its relative position. High Tide has shown resilience in volatile market conditions, maintaining steady growth in its revenue streams. However, its stock price has experienced fluctuations similar to other cannabis stocks, reflecting overall market sentiment and regulatory challenges.

Key Differentiators in Business Model

High Tide’s business model stands out due to its diversified approach, including retail cannabis stores, e-commerce platforms, and proprietary consumption accessories. Key differentiators include:

  • A strong focus on customer loyalty programs, such as the "Cabana Club" membership, which has boosted repeat purchases.
  • A significant presence in international markets, providing a hedge against domestic regulatory risks.
  • Vertical integration through in-house brands for accessories, enhancing profit margins.

Lessons from Industry Leaders

Learning from industry leaders, High Tide can focus on improving operational efficiency and expanding its product portfolio. Companies like Canopy Growth and Tilray have demonstrated the importance of strategic partnerships and acquisitions in scaling operations. High Tide’s strategy of diversifying revenue streams aligns with these practices, yet its smaller market cap suggests room for growth in market share and brand recognition.

While High Tide has made strides in differentiating itself, aligning its strategies with successful industry leaders could unlock further potential.

Future Outlook for High Tide Inc.

Analyst Predictions and Earnings Forecasts

High Tide Inc. has garnered attention from analysts for its potential in the cannabis retail market. While the company has shown resilience in navigating market challenges, forecasts suggest moderate growth in revenue over the next fiscal year. Analysts predict that High Tide’s focus on expanding its e-commerce platform and retail footprint could lead to improved profitability. However, much depends on how well the company can manage operational costs and leverage economies of scale.

Upcoming Catalysts for Stock Movement

Several factors could drive High Tide’s stock performance in the near term:

  1. Expansion into new geographic markets, particularly in regions with evolving cannabis regulations.
  2. Strategic partnerships or acquisitions that enhance its competitive edge.
  3. Regulatory changes that could either open up new opportunities or impose additional compliance costs.

Investors should keep an eye on quarterly earnings reports and any announcements regarding market entry or product innovation.

Risks and Opportunities in the Current Market

The cannabis industry is fraught with both risks and opportunities. For High Tide, the primary risks include:

  • Regulatory uncertainties, especially in international markets.
  • Intense competition leading to margin pressures.
  • Potential delays in achieving profitability due to high operational costs.

On the flip side, opportunities lie in:

  • Growing consumer acceptance of cannabis products.
  • Expansion of its private-label offerings, which could boost margins.
  • Increased adoption of digital sales channels.

The road ahead for High Tide Inc. is a blend of challenges and possibilities. While the company has laid a solid foundation, its success will hinge on strategic execution and adaptability to market dynamics.

Conclusion

In summary, High Tide Inc. continues to be a stock worth monitoring for investors interested in the cannabis retail sector. Its performance reflects both the challenges and opportunities within this evolving market. As always, potential investors should carefully consider their own financial goals and risk tolerance before making any decisions. Staying informed about market trends and company updates will be key to navigating this dynamic space effectively.

data.title: Blaize Holdings: Why I Am Betting On This AI Hardware And Software Small Cap
data.description:
At a recent AI summit in Paris, the CEO of Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Sundar Pichai, mentioned how he thinks “AI will be the most profound shift of our lifetimes”. Sam Altman, OpenAI’s founder, spoke about how he believes “In a decade perhaps everyone on earth will be capable of accomplishing more than the most impactful person can today”.

I can only agree with these observations, as I believe that AI has the potential to disrupt our economic and social fabric to an extent that has not been seen since the industrial revolution.

While I have observed many people thinking AI’s final “shape” is in the form of LLM chatbots such as ChatGPT, I think we have yet to see the most disruptive applications of this new technology in our society. This is why I have been researching the space extensively for the past few years and why I have been investing in Palantir Technologies, Inc. (PLTR) since 2023, covering it on Seeking Alpha since May 2024.

It is with that enthusiasm for AI in mind that, when Seeking Alpha launched its last analyst competition for “Best Small/Micro Cap Idea”, I set out to find new AI companies. That’s because there are no better sectors for finding an asymmetric bet in 2025 than AI, in my view.

After much research, I discuss my findings in this article: a company called Blaize Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:BZAI), which was founded in 2010, but only listed on the Nasdaq in January this year. At the time of writing, and following a share price decline of 60%+ since its listing, I believe BZAI represents an asymmetric bet on AI.

To understand Blaize, you need to understand the AI race

I see two companies standing out in how well they understand what the AI race is all about: NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Palantir. It is perhaps unsurprising then that I find one of the most lucid presentations about AI evolution was delivered by Jensen Huang of Nvidia just a few months ago.

The picture above is taken from that presentation, and outlines the key next steps in the evolution of AI applications according to Nvidia. In short, Nvidia’s vision is for AI to evolve from the current state of Generative AI, which include LLMs such as ChatGPT, to Agentic AI and Physical AI, involving self-driving cars and general robotics (more of my thoughts on AI evolution later in the article).

I think we are currently somewhere between Generative AI and Agentic AI. LLMs, currently widespread and typical of Generative AI, are undergoing a “commoditization”, meaning they are becoming increasingly better and cheaper to operate. These models, today effectively limited to chatbots, will be in my view the base and the “fuel” of Agentic AI - the next step in the AI journey.

I think with Agentic AI we will see accelerated disruptive applications impacting the job market, with many roles - such as executive assistants and customer service jobs - being automated completely. This is something I covered in my last article about Palantir earlier this month.

Today, however, I am not writing about Agentic AI. I am writing about a company which I believe plays in the final stages of the AI race: Physical AI. Let me introduce Blaize.

BZAI is a play on early “Physical AI” applications

I have purposely not mentioned what Blaize core business is until now. The reason is I wanted to make sure readers understand first of all where I think this company fits in the broader context of the AI Race.

What excites me about Blaize is that it is a unique “hardware + software” AI play. The company was founded in 2010 from people in the semiconductor industry and more recently developed an AI software solution called AI Studio. Based on promotional material on the company’s website, I believe AI Studio was launched around or just before 2020, well before the release of ChatGPT triggered a public AI race.

AI Studio, in a nutshell, is a low-to-no-code software that allows users to manage end-to-end “Edge AI” life cycle. In simple words, it is a better User Interface for complex developing jobs in the AI space. The image above, which is taken from a video presentation delivered in 2020, shows the estimated time savings in using AI studio against coding by hand for different AI applications.

AI Studio reminds me of Palantir’s suite of commercial products to some extent, such as Foundry and AIP. Those products are also, at core, a way to effectively integrate AI solutions into existing businesses without the need for complex roll-outs that can take months and thousands of developers’ hours.

However, AI studio is not what I see as Blaize’s main selling point, by far. The company recently launched an AI processor called the Blaize® Pathfinder® P1600, which won the 2022 “product of the year” award by the “Edge AI + Vision alliance”. This is an institution that has existed since 2011 and focuses on “Edge AI”, defined as: “AI processing that occurs locally, whether on a chip, device, or on premise”. It most recently awarded chips released by Qualcomm Incorporated (QCOM) in this same category.

Edge AI chips are used in industrial applications where full blown CPUs or GPUs are not suitable due to excessive cost, excessive consumption, space constraints or a mix of these factors. The image above, taken from another video presenting Blaize’s solutions, outlines exactly how efficiency is one of the main selling points of the company.

One of the prime examples of applications of Edge AI is the automotive sector. With notably low margins, car manufacturers cannot afford to feature commercial or consumer grade CPUs or GPUs in their cars. Not being software houses, they also often struggle to effectively develop full blown software on par with that of Tech companies.

Yet, all automakers today have the need of coming up with increasingly sophisticated AI applications in their cars. These include those needed for Advanced driver-assistance system (ADAS), safety systems (including the operation of radars and cameras) and car infotainment systems. In short, modern cars need increased computational power at a time when car makers undergo cost cutting measures and are not able to attract top talent developers to work on their software. This is where Edge AI comes into play, offering efficient, integrated hardware + software solutions that can run these systems.

Automotive, however, is just the start. Other examples of Edge AI applications include electronic appliances such as smart fridges or factory equipment. In a world where 15 years ago only phones were “smart”, increasingly with AI everything else is becoming smart - from cars to fridges to any type of machine.

Blaize, with Edge AI, is positioned to serve the increasing demand for “Physical AI” applications, which is the last stage of AI applications that Nvidia’s Jensen Huang presented and I discussed earlier in this article.

Understanding Blaize’s product market fit and competitive advantage

In the chart above, I plotted various AI applications and how I think they fit the Technology Adoption Lifecycle. The latter is a tool commonly used in management theory to describe how new technologies are adopted. New technology gets adopted at scale only after a first “early market” of innovators and early adopters pushes said technology to an “early majority” user base. The AI applications spelled out in the chart come from some of my previous work in analyzing the AI space, and are purely a fruit of my own research and thoughts.

I believe LLMs, such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Google’s Gemini, X’s Grok and DeepSeek, are entering a phase of “commoditization” - becoming cheaper and easier to access and integrate over time. These LLMs are already widely used for image editing, image generation, copywriting, translations, internet searches and many other commercial and consumer applications.

While many other analysts and observers believe LLMs are somehow the final or main “shape” of AI, I believe they are just a stepping stone in a game whose ultimate objective is to replace human input in the workforce altogether. In this context, I plotted LLMs on the center / right side of the chart, as I believe they are already entering their “late majority” phase of the adoption cycle.

Other AI applications, such as adoption in CRM or Supply Chain, are in my view still in the “early majority” of the AI product lifecycle. While companies such as Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) have already deployed AI solutions in their suite of products, much remains to be done in terms of adoption.

I see Virtual AI assistants as one of the applications that is shifting from an early adopter to an early majority user base - something that I expect will happen with the troubled launch of Apple Inc (AAPL)’s AI assistant.

“Edge AI”, which is the subject of this article as it directly concerns Blaize, is still in an “early adopters” phase, in my opinion. While smart fridges and smart cars already exist, I believe we will see a drastic acceleration in how smart and connected these devices will become in the next decade or so.

Blaize is far from being the only company playing in this space (something I will cover in more detail in the risk section of this article). However, I believe it is playing in the right segment: an up-and-coming AI application that has still a lot to grow.

There are also several elements that, in my view, constitute a competitive advantage of this company and make me think they can deliver on Edge AI:

  1. The company was focusing on software, and only relatively recently pivoted to hardware. I believe this is essential as Edge AI can only work if hardware and software work together seamlessly.

  2. Blaize has a long history, having been founded in 2010. It only very recently listed on the Nasdaq via a SPAC deal.

  3. Its top management features several names with decades in the microchip and Tech industry.

By far Blaize’s main competitive advantage in my view is that it was focusing on software up until relatively recently, only launching its flagship microchip in 2022. Software is incredibly important in Edge AI (and in general, in all AI applications). One of the reasons why AMD GPUs are often behind Nvidia’s in testing is that Nvidia is better at developing software than AMD.

While software is important in the context of data centers, it is even more crucial in the context of AI microchips that find their ways into cars or electric appliances, where efficiency is key. Blaize is very keen on promoting efficiency as one of their main selling points, stating the following about automotive applications:

Replace power-hungry GPU and FPGA chips with Blaize® GSP-based platforms for up to 60x systems efficiency advantage and a single scalable architecture across use cases.

A hardware + software solution is not only a matter of mere efficiency. It is a matter of having a strong selling proposition. In approaching a legacy automaker, or a legacy maker of electric appliances, it is essential that Blaize can market a “turnkey” solution. I believe these legacy producers will soon be struggling to adapt to the AI age, where all of their products need to become “smart”, fast. Having previously focused on the mere engineering of their machines, legacy producers will struggle to shift to an age where it’s all about microchips and software.

An example of this struggle in the automotive world is that of Porsche Automobil Holding SE (OTCPK:POAHY). This prestigious carmaker is struggling with its new electric cars, with the EV Taycan being subject to multiple recalls due to software and hardware issues, and the launch of the EV Macan delayed for similar reasons.

In this context, I expect a strong demand for turnkey solutions such as that of Blaize, where a customer doesn’t need to invest in overhead (developers, software experts) to advance in the AI age.

In terms of management and company history, Blaize’s co-founders all share a past working for Intel and Nvidia. The company was founded in 2010 with the original name of “ThinCI, Inc” and it enjoyed significant funding as a startup from a syndicate of investors in the automotive space. This, in my view, signals how Blaize is serious about automotive applications and it can start hitting the ground running in this space - something I will cover shortly, in the next section of my article.

Some readers may find this overall story familiar: a Tech company that after many years as a private business, playing primarily in one industry, becomes public and starts focusing on AI. This is roughly the pattern that Palantir followed, having been a private company operating primarily in the military industry from 2003 to 2020. Of course, Palantir and Blaize are vastly different companies and I am in no way suggesting that Blaize will follow Palantir’s playbook or success simply because of its history. However, I think having a solid background and history represents a significant validation in a space, that of SPACs, which is often riddled with borderline fraud.

I have a $ 12 price target for BZAI

At the time of its Nasdaq listing, Blaize is reporting negligible revenue and a negative Operating Income of $ 48 Million. The company also has $ 68.6 Million in cash (Seeking Alpha data). It is relatively unsurprising, then, that the company lost more than half of its market capitalization since its listing in early January.

However, I think this has been a fundamental mistake by the market, which has treated Blaize as yet another SPAC game of smoke and mirrors on the Nasdaq. The company has actually recently reported some reassuring revenue and profit guidance, as follows:

  • Expected revenue of $19M to $50M in FY 2025, increasing to $105M to $140M in FY 2026.

  • Non-GAAP gross margins projected between 46% to 50% for both FY 2025 and FY 2026.

The above guidance was also what, in my view, triggered the stock’s bounce from the bottom: BZAI’s stock has gone up by more than 60% from its low of $ 3 per share, trading in the $ 5 range at the time of writing this article.

Assuming a midpoint range of the above guidance and taking into consideration the expenses the company released along with this guidance, Blaize is set to burn roughly ~$ 50 Million in 2025:

  • Revenue: Midpoint of $19M - $50M = $34.5M
  • Total Expenses (Midpoint): $50.5M (R&D) + $33M (SG&A) = $83.5M
  • Operating Profit: Revenue ($34.5M) - Total Expenses ($83.5M) = -$49M

For fiscal year 2026, the company is set to roughly break-even, when taking into consideration midpoint estimates:

  • Revenue: Midpoint of $105M - $140M = $122.5M
  • Total Expenses (Midpoint): $87M (R&D) + $36M (SG&A) = $123M
  • Operating Profit: Revenue ($122.5M) - Total Expenses ($123M) = -$0.5M

Assuming a generous P/S ratio of 10 and 2026 Midpoint revenue, the company could be worth $ 1.225 billion based on 2026 figures ($122.5M * 10). That would be equivalent to $ 17.75 per share, without accounting for any share dilution. Considering I do expect the company to issue new shares as many early stage Tech companies do, my target is $ 12 for BZAI. This represents a 2.2X+ upside potential from the current price of roughly $ 5 per share.

Note that I am not considering any P/E metric in my valuation model, as I think it would be too premature given the early stages the company is at. The guidance on gross margins of 40% to 50% reassures me that the business is sustainable and will eventually turn out very profitable past 2026.

Of course, the figures in my valuation model may vary considerably. Should BZAI manage to deliver on the top-end range of its guidance, the company may turn significantly profitable in 2026 and at a P/S of 10 may be worth up to $ 30 per share. This, along with other scenarios and catalysts, is what I will cover next.

BZAI: What Catalysts to look out for next

The fundamental issue with BZAI is that the company has a lot to prove to shareholders. Its original listing price, at around $ 12, seems rich considering the lack of revenue. While I do believe the company can climb back to that valuation, there are several catalysts that I will closely monitor to understand whether BZAI is really a good AI play. These include:

  • Real commercial contracts starting to flow in, making the company’s guidance appear realistic and based on facts, not hope.
  • The company expanding beyond the automotive industry, which seems to be its core competency but also represents a relatively stagnant and margin-compressed industry.
  • The company starting to eventually display a “Rule of 40” of above 40, as many great AI companies do.
  • The “commoditization of LLMs”, i.e. AI models becoming exponentially cheaper to operate and easier to integrate into any software solutions, including those of Blaize.

With regards to the commoditization of LLMs, I want to spend a few more words on this subject as I do believe it could be a strong catalyst that might dramatically accelerate the adoption of Edge AI.

The release of DEEPSEEK, a Chinese LLM, has recently brought a lot of noise in the AI industry, even triggering a sell-off of Nvidia shares. I think the DeepSeek news itself was overblown, and I do not trust the official facts that it was developed without access to advanced microchips and with only $ 6 million in budget. This said, I do believe DeepSeek is a step in the direction of LLMs becoming cheaper and “commoditized”. This is consistent with what Nvidia’s once again foresees, as displayed by the image above. Computational power is exponentially rising with the last of Nvidia’s chips, while energy required to operate them is decreasing exponentially.

I see this trend as a positive catalyst for the overall AI industry, because resources can be allocated to developing more sophisticated AI models, such as AI agents, rather than mere chatbots. Ultimately, I expect the final goal of this AI race to be to automate the entirety of human input in the labor market with more efficient artificial input.

In the context of Blaize, I would expect LLMs becoming cheaper to represent a tailwind for the company. Eventually, the objective in terms of Edge AI should be to sell turnkey microchips with AI models that allow for full automation to cars or electric appliances. This would allow Blaize’s customers to fully outsource AI needs, and focus once again on the engineering behind their products, rather than software.

While this scenario is far-fetched based on today’s technology, I wouldn’t be surprised if it manifests sooner rather than later, and provides a strong tailwind to companies in the Edge AI space like Blaize.

Risks: fierce competition and a lot to prove

I want to be clear with readers that investing in Blaize comes at significant risks. The company is reporting negligible revenue at the time of writing, and my entire bullish case rests on guidance that was recently provided by the company itself.

The main risk with investing in Blaize is exactly that the company might not report the expected level of revenue. Their flagship microchip was launched 2 full years ago, yet the company is not registering significant revenue yet. This fact is my single largest doubt about Blaize, which is only partially offset by their strong ties with the automotive industry that make me trust they will start selling soon.

The other considerable risk with investing in Blaize concerns competition. Blaize is by far not the only, or even the most relevant, player in the Edge AI space. Large players like Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) are all already serving this space, including the automotive industry. AMD’s Embedded segment - which include the automotive industry - is worth just shy of $ 1 Billion as of Q4 earnings. Nvidia’s automotive and robotics segment is worth $ 449 Million as of Q3 2024, growing at 30% on a quarterly basis.

Further competition might soon arise from Chinese microchip producers. While Chinese automakers are still relying for the vast majority of their microchip needs on imports, things may change fast. China’s first automotive grade certified chip was released just last November. Should the Chinese manage to catch up on this segment, this would prove an incredibly powerful headwind for Blaize, potentially squeezing their otherwise healthy 40% to 50% margins.

While I believe the company is positioned to cut itself a niche in this growing Edge AI market, doubts remain, based on a quickly evolving industry with fierce competition.

Another element of risk concerns the over-reliance of Blaize to the automotive industry. A global slowdown in automotive sales may affect the adoption of AI technology in new cars. And the industry may be especially subject to fierce competition from companies that can provide better prices.

Ultimately, my bullish case for Blaize primarily stems from the fact that the stock was subject to a heavy market correction after its listing. Were the stock to still be valued at $ 1+ Billion in market capitalization, an asymmetric investment opportunity would not present itself, today.

Conclusion: BZAI is an asymmetric bet on Edge AI

Blaize sells an efficient AI processor which, coupled with the company’s proprietary software, won a 2022 “product of the year” award in Edge AI. Edge AI is one of the nascent applications in the AI race, part of what Nvidia’s Jensen Huang defines as “Physical AI”. We are talking about “smart” and efficient microchips that power machinery that - for matters of size, efficiency or cost - cannot rely on a full blown CPU or GPU. Industrial include those in automotive, electric appliances and more generally any IoT device.

The competition for Blaize is fierce. Incumbent players, including AMD and Nvidia, all compete to serve the promising Edge AI market. While Chinese players are not in the picture for now, at least in automotive, new Chinese competitors may rise soon, too.

Yet, I believe we are in front of an asymmetric opportunity. BZAI’s market capitalization - at only $ 500 Million at the time of writing following a correction - is minuscule compared to the sheer size of the potential demand for Edge AI solutions. The company also seems to be at a heavy discount against its revenue and profit guidance that was released only a few days ago.

All Blaize has to do for the stock to more than double from current levels is to demonstrate it has a decent product that can participate in the Edge AI game, and prove its guidance is based on facts. While that in itself is a risk, I believe Blaize’s team of industry professionals and recent news about supposed product momentum are worth taking a bet. I rate the stock a "STRONG BUY", with a $ 12 price target based on company's guidance.

Personally, I purchased 2,000 shares in BZAI and I will closely monitor the company for future catalysts that may signal whether or not Blaize has what it takes to cut itself some room in the AI race. I plan to cover the company again in the future based on these observations.

Fast Retailing (OTCPK:FRCOF)(OTCPK:FRCOY). James Hardie Industries (JHX)(OTCPK:JHIUF). We exited our position in this construction materials company due to concerns about its declining earnings growth.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. The firm continues to benefit from growing global semiconductor demand. The company’s confidence in its capacity plans is backed up by customer demand related to long-term megatrends, in our view.

SAP. The enterprise application software company stands to benefit from strong momentum in its cloud computing business and the secular artificial intelligence(AI) theme, which management believes will be transformative for the company.

Schneider Electric. The firm benefits from the demand for electrical grid improvements and greater efficiency of electrical systems. We believe Schneider could also benefit from increased investment to upgrade the grid and systems to accommodate electric vehicles and hybrids.

London Stock Exchange Group. The financial exchange has a strong balance sheet, has demonstrated business improvement and is growing business in an area of financials we believe is more shielded from the risks of higher-for-longer interest rates facing banks and other financial institutions.

Novo Nordisk. We believe the pharmaceutical company should continue to see accelerating growth trends due to the launch of Rybelsus, which is used to treat Type 2 diabetes, the approval of semaglutide(the chemical name of Rybelsus) to treat obesity and a full phase 3 product pipeline.

Hitachi. We believe Hitachi’s information technology services business will see strong growth as Japanese companies continue to catch up in digital investment. Its power grid division should benefit from AI-related power demand, while the building system division should benefit from infrastructure spending.

Barclays. We expect the company’s earnings to improve, driven by more supportive market conditions for the investment bank and higher net interest margins. We also expect greater profitability in its credit card business, which should benefit from slower investment spending.

Tencent Holdings. We think Tencent’s revenue growth will accelerate due to the Chinese government's supportive actions, which should improve the overall economy and help the company’s advertising revenue growth. China’s regulatory environment has also relaxed recently, and its approval process for video games has resumed.

NTT Data Group. We believe the company’s earnings growth is well positioned to benefit from accelerating demand for digitalization and data center capacity. The cyclical recovery in information technology consulting in Japan is also supportive.

Keyence. Keyence is Japan’s leading domestic supplier of sensors, measuring equipment, vision systems and programmable logic controllers. We believe Keyence is well positioned to benefit from a broad range of manufacturing trends such as increased quality control, traceability and machine guidance.

data.title: Little Known DXP Enterprises Checks A Lot Of Peter Lynch 'Perfect Stock' Boxes
data.description:
Value is hard… very, very h-a-r-d.

Computing the necessary ratios is easy.

One challenge comes in recognizing which stocks are cheap because they deserve to be cheap. We know how to evaluate companies. We know what fundamental and growth metrics to study.

The real problem comes from them… the countless other investors looking at the same information. The 1930s, when Graham and Dodd did their thing, are long gone.

So, good companies are usually recognized as such. Their stocks accordingly carry high valuations. Corporate dregs are likewise readily identified. Those are the ones with low valuations.

We need the seldom-found needles in haystacks… good companies that are unrecognized by the market, and hence, have stocks with meagre valuations.

Peter Lynch, the legendary long-ago manager of the Fidelity Magellan mutual fund, gives us some things to consider.

Chapter 8 of his classic One Up on Wall Street is titled “The Perfect Stock, What a Deal!” It lists 13 things to look for. I doubt any company ever had or will ever have all 13 qualities.

But tiny (about $1.5 billion in market cap) DXP Enterprises (NASDAQ: DXPE) checks seven of those boxes. They are, quoting from Lynch’s Chapter 8 sub-headings…

  • It Sounds Dull — or, Even Better, Ridiculous
  • It Does Does Something Dull
  • The Institutions Don’t Own It, And the Analysts Don’t Follow It
  • People Have to Keep Buying It
  • It’s A User of Technology
  • The Insiders are Buyers
  • The Company is Buying back Shares

An important theme here is that shares of a worthy company can be modestly valued because of apathy; inattention. I agree. But I want to add one more factor on my own…

  • There’s Reason to Believe the Company Will Attract More Attention in the Future

This factor, let’s call it a catalyst, is important.

Modestly valued stocks are great to buy. But ideally, we want them to become at least fairly valued, and preferably overvalued, when we want to sell. So we want to see a reason for things to change.

Note, though, that Lynch’s book debuted in 1989.

The world and the market changed a lot even since then.

So today, it’s probably unrealistic for companies to meet the strict letter of the law for each test. But DXPE meets the letter for five of them. For the other two, it meets the spirit of the law. And it satisfies the test I added.

So now, let’s get to know this promising but thus-far inconspicuous company.

Introducing DXPE’s Businesses

Simply put, DXPE is an industrial products distributor.

It has three divisions… Service Centers (SC), Innovative Pumping Solutions (IPS), and Supply Chain Services (SCS).

Herer’s an overview of the product portfolio.

Here are visuals from the page 6 of the 2023 10-K. (The 2024 numbers, when reported, will be pretty similar.).

You may not love oil and gas being such a conspicuous part of DXPE’s business. DXPE didn’t love that either. So it’s been working hard over the past ten years to better diversify itself.

Here are the latest numeric segment breakdowns.

Whoa! You’ve just started to nod off, didn’t you.

That’s OK. Remember Lynch’s first two cues to the perfect stock. They both dealt with monotony.

So we’re vigorously checking the first two Lynch boxes! I hope that motivates you to stay with me.

If that’s not enough, I’ll tell you now the coming attractions look better… Customers need to buy from DXPE, it’s a user of technology (including the big one… AI), and there are catalysts for future growth.

Back, for now, to basics…

  • (1) Service Centers

This division’s bailiwick is in MRO (Maintenance, Repair and Operations) products. It distributes for more than 1 million items. (Source: 10-K, page 7.)

The items are as dull as dull can be… “rotating equipment, bearing, power transmission, hose, fluid power, metal working, industrial supply and safety product and service categories…. (from 10-K page 7).

But if one of these snooze-producers wears down, life can get quite frantic. Industrial processes can stop. Production deadlines get missed. Companies can report disappointing results and/or guidance. Stockholders can go ballistic…

Little boring things can spark big harsh drama.

So DXPE checks another Peter Lunch factor box… customers have to keep buying!

That said, the economy ebbs and flows. So, too do interest rates and inflation. Customers add and close facilities.

So nobody can guarantee that each year will be better than the last. (In fact, 2024 was lackluster.) But in a big picture sense, customers must keep buying.

Still, this is a competitive business. Why should customers buy from DXPE? And will more buyers turn to DXPE in the future?

  • (2) Innovative Pumping Solutions

This is presently a small part of DXPE. But as you can see from the above segment tables, it’s growing fast.

Its product offerings sound almost as dull as the MRO collection. But don’t let that fool you.

There’s a saying that water is the new oil. It’s irreplaceable. It’s becoming harder to get and continue supplying clean water. And there’s been underinvestment in water infrastructure.

So as sleepy as things like “pumps, motors, valves, and consumable products such as welding supplies” (10-K, page 7) sound, we need them. And they need to be replaced when worn.

We’re back to customers need to buy them. That’s why DXPE is working to build itself up in this business.

  • (3) Supply Chain Services

Is this as dull as pumping and MRO? That’s a matter of opinion.

Traditionally, I can understand it being sleep inducing. But that may no longer be the case.

The pandemic turned “supply chain” into a hip phrase. It was often said to be blocked, slow, clogged, etc. And if you’ve been reading conference call transcripts, you probably notice it’s still at times cited as a reason results and/or guidance have been meh.

Distributors are part of the supply chain… and then some.

Phrases like value added distributors have been used to describe “companies that go beyond the traditional role of wholesale distribution by offering additional services …. such as product customization, technical support, product training, and marketing assistance.”

It’s related to a long-developing outsourcing trend in which companies “focus on the core aspects of the business, spinning off the less critical operations to outside organizations.”

This part of DXPE focuses here. Specifically, according to page 8 of the 10-K:

The Company's Supply Chain Services (SCS) segment manages all or part of its customers’ supply chains, including procurement and inventory management. The SCS segment enters into long-term contracts with its customers that can be canceled on little or no notice under certain circumstances. The SCS segment provides fully outsourced MRO solutions for sourcing MRO products including, but not limited to, the following: inventory optimization and management; store room management; transaction consolidation and control; vendor oversight and procurement cost optimization; productivity improvement services; and customized reporting.

SCS is on DXPE’s books as its smallest segment in terms of revenues and operating profit. But undoubtedly, it’s existence helps make the Service Center division bigger than it would otherwise be.

Growth Prospects

We can look at this three ways.

  • (1) Run of the Mill (Cyclical) Growth

This is pretty basic.

As equipment is used, things wear out. Wearing out is related to usage. And usage reflects economic activity. That generates basic cyclical industrial distribution demand.

A good economy in 2025 should help DXPE post better results.

  • (2) External Growth – Acquisitions

Industrial distribution is a very fragmented business. That means the industry is dominated by many small-time players.

DXPE explains, on page 5 of its 10-k:

Most industrial customers currently purchase their industrial supplies through numerous local distribution and supply companies. These distributors generally provide the customer with repair and maintenance services, technical support and application expertise with respect to one product category. Products typically are purchased by the distributor for resale directly from the manufacturer and warehoused at distribution facilities of the distributor until sold to the customer. The customer also typically will purchase an amount of product inventory for its near term anticipated needs and store those products at its industrial site until the products are used.

(Emphasis supplied.)

Just reading that calls to mind massive inefficiency! Excess inventories are the bane of every corporate treasurer’s existence. And shortages… stockouts… let’s not go there.

This is why large outfits are so important. (DXPE says it ranked number 17 in the U.S. at the end of 2022.)

Scale lets the DXPEs of the world manage their own inventories more tightly. And that same scale gives customers the confidence they need to manage their own stocks more efficiently (i.e. buy when needed instead of stockpiling so much ahead of time).

“JIT” (Just in Time) inventory isn’t new. I learned about it in business school in the 1970s. But scaling up makes companies better than ever at doing it.

Now, you may be inclined to think it’s odd to encounter of a $1.5 billion company like DXPE together with the word “scale.”

Think again. DXPE is scaling up by making acquisitions… a lot of them.

That can sound scary. Every acquisition brings with it risk of consequences if the buyer overpays and/or if it doesn’t smoothly integrate new and existing operations.

Traditionally, serial acquirers have often been criticized. Lately, there’s been more tolerance of the practice. But investors always need to stay on their guard.

We should be especially vigilant when a tiny company like DXPE is using debt to acquire. I’ll address that below.

We’ll see below that DXPE is a sensible acquirer.

That’s important. A successful ongoing acquisition program will play a big role in giving DXPE the sale it needs to really get cooking…

Before I go on, I can’t resist share the “nice dogs” gem uttered by CEO David Little on the 11/5/24 earnings call.

While discussion acquisitions, the question arose as to whether DXPE also sells divisions. Little pointed out that DXPE buys businesses to own and run them. It doesn’t look to trade in and out of them.

But he couldn’t and wouldn’t rule it out. (That makes sense since one table below will show that divestitures are not always zero.) So, he maintains, prospective sellers see DXPE as a desirable acquirer.

Little noted that there might be a fit that doesn’t work. But it wouldn’t be selling a division because it’s bad. In his words…

[E]verything we are doing makes pretty decent money. And so it’s not like we are trying to sell something that’s just a dog over here, and so we don’t have any dogs. If we do, they are all really nice dogs.

  • (3) Technologically-spurred Growth

Peter Lynch listed user of technology as a perfect-stock indicator.

Industrial distribution is a remarkably, if under-publicized and under-discussed, user of technology.

If you Google the phrase “maintenance,” you’ll quickly run into four kinds of activity… corrective, preventive, predictive and proactive.

Corrective is what most people think of… Wait for something to break. Then, fix it.

The other three categories are more attuned to today’s hyper competitive cost conscious industrial world. Winning companies are those that get ahead of problems.

And when we say such things as preventive, predictive and proactive… what comes to mind?

Answer: Digital technology… including you-know-what (AI).

DXPE is very wired into this evolving world. It’s pushing four elements of digital supply chain transformation.

Internet of Things (IOT)…

provides real-time visibility and insights into the movement of goods and resources. IoT solutions use sensors to collect data that manufacturers can use to better monitor the steps involved in the production and distribution of a product.

Artificial Intelligence (AI)…

enables businesses to optimize operations, reduce costs, and improve customer satisfaction. With hyper-automation, embedded AI, and analytics, AI is creating a new approach to modern supply chain digitization.

By leveraging AI and machine learning, businesses can gain insights into their operations and make more informed decisions. Additionally, AI-enabled asset-tracking solutions can help companies to reduce costs and improve their supply chain operations.

Blockchain technology…

(helps) businesses can ensure the traceability and security of their supply chain operations, leading to increased transparency and efficiency.

The cloud…

(helps) companies… seamlessly integrate various aspects of their supply chain, from procurement and production to distribution and logistics. This real-time integration enables enhanced visibility, collaboration, and data-sharing among stakeholders, breaking down silos and streamlining communication.

With data accessible from anywhere at any time, decision-makers can make informed choices based on accurate, up-to-date information, leading to better forecasting, optimized inventory management, and more agile responses to market fluctuations.

DXPE’s Supply Chain Services division has already…

invested in a customer care model, allowing customers to utilize DXP’s remote technology within the need for full – without the need of full-time on-site presence. This model enables DXP to extend Supply Chain Services technology to accounts with smaller sites and expand the business relationship.

There’s a long way to go. But DXPE is pushing digital services to customers. Additional scale will help.

And this should help DXPE outgrow basic industrial activity going forward. As a user of technology DXPE should be increasingly able to attract more business from the many that have not yet started outsourcing to first-tier distributors such as itself.

As such things start to make their way to the income statement, they should serve as catalysts to push the stock’s valuation upward.

And by the way, the reference to which I linked regarding DXPE’s tech efforts comes from content DXPE aims at customers and prospective customers.

Management doesn’t even mention “AI” in its shareholder materials. That hype-free investor communication approach is refreshing.

DXPE’s Financial Profile – The Numbers

Let’s now see what the basic numbers look like.

We’ll start with quarterly trends.

The 2024 third quarter, paced by big project bookings relating to energy and water/wastewater, won’t persist. Ex that, 2024 wasn’t a great year.

But we see below that it wasn’t great for the industry as a whole.

(I compare DXPE to medians among companies in the Trading Companies and Distributors industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Because DXPE is small, I also compare it to the SPDR Portfolio S&P 600 Small Cap ETF (SPSM). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

Focus on the EBITDA YOY row. That profit figure is less impacted by accounting oddities than (GAAP) EPS. Compared to benchmarks, DXPE gave a good account of itself during the off year.

Here are the forward expectations.

That looks good.

The next table, showing the core fundamentals, is especially important, considering the importance to DXPE of frequent acquisitions.

There are two things we must key on.

One consists of the profitability (company returns) metrics in the bottom two rows. Badly conceived or executed acquisition programs hit companies here.

DXPE’s returns are fine!

The top four rows deal with financial strength. The reaction here is “Hmm…”

It’s not necessarily negative. Interest coverage is positive; and above 2.00. But comparatively speaking, this, and the other metrics in this part of the table, signal us to look more closely.

We’ll do that in the next section.

Risk

Let’s dive right into the financial highlights. Acquisitions, debt… we need a closer look.

I’m going to start from the lower portion of the table and work my way up.

Right away, I note that DXPE has been buying back shares! That, as noted above, is one of the characteristics Peter Lynch favors.

What’s interesting here is how small DXPE is, and the debt-related ratios we saw in the fundamentals table presented in the last section.

Looking from right (recent years) to left, we see that neither debt changes nor changes in equity are steady. We see borrowing moving up a bit more to acquisitions … it seems more opportunistic than pattern based.

I like this. It indicates DXPE, however much acquisitions figure in its strategy, aren’t being made for the heck of it. This is consistent with the idea of a company that acquires when the candidate is right, and when the price is right.

(Were DXPE to have been careless in either regard, we’d most likely see it reflected in bad company returns. That’s not the case here.)

Looking at the large numbers for new debt and acquisitions in the Latest period, it looks like we’re seeing a confluence of opportunity. We’re not seeing signs of a company that’s simply running up the M&A tab.

The company did call out the significance of recent acquisitions on the latest earnings call. So big number for the latest period make sense.

But look now at this table.

Sales per business day is a metric management tracks and discusses during its earnings calls. They’re rising. That’s nice.

Look, though, at the breakdowns between organic and acquisitions-based numbers.

Organic dominates… by a lot.

(And check the footnote at the bottom of this table. Operation A might be booked as Acquisition Sales now, but after it’s on board for 12 months, it graduates to Organic.)

All in all DXPEE looks like a company whose capital allocation strategy (among acquisitions, share repurchases and debt) is very deliberate… attuned to where the business and the opportunities and conditions happen to be.

Things are under control!

That’s what I want to see from a small cap firm going through an acquisitions-oriented strategy like this.

Moving further up the table, we see the debt ratio is stable.

In the income area, we see stable gross margin. And, lately, the operating margin has been rising. That suggests improving coverage of fixed operating costs.

We’re back again to that word “control.” That’s a good word. It’s a risk moderating word!

Now, speaking of control, I have to air a bit of dirty DXPE laundry… but dirty laundry that’s been thoroughly and successfully cleaned up.

I’m talking about accounting… issues relating to effective control over internal policies that dictate when sales may or may not be booked.

Shades of Super Micro Computer(SMCI) and it 10-K that is nearly 180 days overdue. I went ballistic about such problems for SMCI. See here. (I expect to write about it again after the 10-K get filed, potentially very soon.)

But I’m calm about DXPE.

The latter differs starkly from SMCI. The latter’s situation dragged on for a long time, resulted in issues with NASDAQ listing, the SEC, and even the Justice department. And it culminated in a very nasty resignation by Ernst & Young (EY), SMCI’s now-ex bule chip accounting firm.

DXPE, on the other hand has been jumping all over the problem, tightening up as necessary, disclosing generously in is SEC filings, and working closely with its former and current accounting firms. The latter, PriceWaterhouseCoopers (PWC), is also blue chip like EY.

(You can read all the details if you wish on pages 42-45 of DXPE’s 12/31/23 10-K and page 29 of the 9/30/24 10-Q. I’m satisfied with what I read.)

What to do About DXPE Stock

Here are the basic valuation numbers.

This isn’t super-duper cigar-butt value.

But it’s far cry from the 50+ or even 100+ and sometimes 150+ P/Es we’re seeing on today hottest growth companies.

DXPE isn’t likely to post the sort of growth we’re expecting from the big-tech leaders. But it is much, much, much easier to look at the valuation numbers and conclude that DXPE’s stock price is very reasonable in light of what the company is.

Note, too, the PEG FWD I show is probably much too high. Because there is no Proj. 3-5Y EPS Gr figure available for DXPE, I just plugged in the industry median.

But DXPE has a track record of substantially outgrowing its industry. If it continues to do likewise in the future, that would suggest a higher Proj. 3-5Y EPS Gr and a lower PEG FWD.

And, by the way, there are two other Peter Lynch factors worth mentioning here.

In this day and age, it’s probably unrealistic to expect any legitimate company to have zero institutional ownership and zero analytic coverage.

But DXPE has only once covering analyst.

And it’s under-owned by institutions.

That gives DXPE what Lynch wants from his coverage/ownership factor… Ample room for the stock and its valuation to rise as the company grows and more investors start paying attention… and buying.

The above table also shows insider ownership.

Lynch and many others like to see this (Lynch said insider buying. I don’t have comparative data on that. So I’ll look instead at ownership percent.)

Insider ownership at DXPE is pretty high by public company standards. So that checks another important Lynch box… insiders whose interests are aligned with those of public shareholders.

Here’s the price chart.

That’s a good chart!

The price and the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) are well above the 50-day EMA. and kept on rising.

And how ‘bout those Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and Chaikin Oscillator (CO) indicators.

Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both have been spending a lot of time in bullish territory. That means buyers have been a lot more motivated than sellers. That exerts upward pressure on stock prices.

Nobody can keep inhaling any stock forever. Both may have to take breathers soon. CO seems to be edging in that direction right now. But on the whole, this is a very strong CMF-CO profile.

As I’ve said before, my investment stance depends mainly on whether I think a stock will be better than, in line with, or worse than market.

Here’s how I apply that to the Seeking Alpha rating system:

  • “Strong Buy” means I see the stock as being better than the market and I’m bullish about the direction of the market.
  • “Buy” means I see the stock as being better than the market but am not confident about the market’s near-term direction.
  • “Hold” means I see the stock as moving in line with the market.
  • “Sell” means I see the stock as being worse than the market but am not confident about the market’s near-term direction.
  • “Strong Sell” means I see the stock as being worse than the market and I’m bearish about the direction of the market.

Based on this scale, I’m rating DXPE as a “Buy.”
